worth_it_explanation: Retail conversion tracking is the foundation of every Amazon DSP campaign evaluation. Without it, the campaign reports impressions and clicks but nothing that connects spend to outcomes. The checks here are procedural: they confirm that the correct conversion events are selected in the Amazon DSP console, that the Amazon Ad Tag pixel fires on any off-Amazon conversion pages, and that promoted Amazon Standard Identification Numbers (ASINs) are mapped to the line items that drive them. Each check is a console or pixel inspection, not a technical rebuild, and any gap found here will immediately improve reported performance once corrected.
intro: Amazon DSP campaigns report conversions in two ways depending on the advertiser type. Endemic advertisers, meaning those who sell products on Amazon.com, receive retail conversion signals, such as ASIN purchases, detail page views, and add-to-cart events, directly from Amazon's purchase graph. Non-endemic advertisers, those driving traffic to destinations off Amazon.com, must implement the Amazon Ad Tag pixel on their own pages to give Amazon DSP a conversion signal to optimize toward. These checks confirm that the right conversion events are active, that the pixel is in place where it is required, and that the promoted ASINs are mapped correctly so conversion data attributes to the products the campaign is actually pushing.

audit_checks.title: Retail conversion events are not selected on endemic advertiser line items
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When no retail conversion events are selected, Amazon DSP has no purchase signal to optimize toward. The campaign runs on click or viewability proxies rather than actual shopping behavior, and the reporting shows no purchase data, so neither the bidding nor the post-campaign analysis reflects real retail outcomes.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP provides endemic advertisers, those who sell products on Amazon.com, with retail conversion signals that come directly from Amazon's purchase graph. These signals include ASIN purchases, detail page views (DPV), and add-to-cart (ATC) events. Unlike third-party pixels, these events do not require any tag on the advertiser's own pages. They are available at the order and line item level in the Amazon DSP console, and they must be actively selected as the conversion goals for a campaign to optimize toward them and include them in conversion reporting.

When no retail conversion event is selected, the line item runs without a purchase signal. Amazon DSP may still optimize toward engagement or click proxies depending on the buying strategy, but those proxies do not reflect whether impressions drove purchases. The campaign's performance reporting will show no purchase conversions, which makes it impossible to calculate return on ad spend (ROAS) or assess new-to-brand (NTB) impact.

The setting lives at the order and line item level in the Amazon DSP console. You may want to confirm it on every active line item, not just at the order level. Line items can override the order-level selection. A few signs suggest this check applies:
  • The campaign performance report shows zero purchase conversions despite active spend
  • The line item optimization goal is set to a click or reach objective rather than a purchase or DPV goal
  • The order-level conversion goal is set but no purchase signal appears in line item reporting
You may want to treat missing purchase conversions in the report as the first signal to check the conversion event selection rather than interpreting them as true zero-conversion performance.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an endemic brand running product detail page campaigns, DPV, ATC, and purchase events are all meaningful signals. You may want to confirm that at minimum the purchase event is selected. DPV and ATC add optimization signal earlier in the funnel and can improve performance on prospecting line items targeting cold audiences.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: Non-endemic advertisers driving to off-Amazon lead generation pages do not have access to Amazon's retail conversion signals. This check applies only to endemic advertisers. For non-endemic campaigns, see the Amazon Ad Tag check in this subcategory.

learn_more.source_title: Introduction to Amazon Attribution and campaign reporting

investigate.intro: Confirm that at least one retail conversion event, such as purchase, DPV, or ATC, is selected on each active line item for endemic advertiser campaigns.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the Amazon DSP console and navigate to the advertiser account.

Open the order for the campaign you are reviewing and inspect the conversion goals set at the order level. Note which retail conversion events are selected, such as purchase, detail page view, or add to cart.

Open each active line item under that order and confirm whether the line item carries a conversion goal. Line items can override or not inherit the order-level selection, so check each one individually.

In the performance report for the line item, filter for the purchase conversions column. A column that shows dashes or zeros throughout the flight while spend is active suggests no purchase event is selected or the event is selected but no promoted ASIN is mapped.

evaluate.logic: For each active line item on an endemic advertiser order, the practitioner records which retail conversion event is selected and whether the performance report shows purchase conversion data. A line item with at least one retail conversion event selected, such as purchase, DPV, or ATC, and a non-zero purchase column in the report passes. A line item with no retail conversion event selected, or with a retail event selected but zero purchase conversions throughout the flight, is a fail recorded with the order name, line item name, and the conversion column value observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the conversion event selection and the purchase conversion column value for each active line item above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more active line items on {{site_name}}'s Amazon DSP endemic campaigns do not have retail conversion events selected. Without a purchase, detail page view, or add-to-cart signal selected as the conversion goal, the campaign optimizes without any retail outcome signal and purchase conversions do not appear in reporting, making return on ad spend calculations impossible. {{voice_recommend}} opening each affected line item in the Amazon DSP console, selecting the appropriate retail conversion event, and confirming that purchase conversions appear in the line item performance report within the next reporting window.

audit_checks.title: Amazon Ad Tag pixel is not implemented on non-endemic advertiser conversion pages
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without the Amazon Ad Tag pixel, Amazon DSP has no conversion signal from the advertiser's off-Amazon pages. The campaign cannot optimize toward the off-Amazon outcome the advertiser cares about, and post-campaign reporting shows no conversion data, so there is no basis for evaluating whether the spend drove results.

educate.base:
Non-endemic advertisers are brands whose conversion happens off Amazon.com, such as an automotive company driving to a configurator page, a financial services brand driving to an application form, or a direct-to-consumer brand driving to its own storefront. Because these conversions do not appear in Amazon's purchase graph, non-endemic campaigns rely on the Amazon Ad Tag, a JavaScript pixel placed on the advertiser's own conversion pages, to pass conversion signals back to Amazon DSP.

The Amazon Ad Tag must be placed on every page that represents a conversion goal for the campaign, such as a thank-you page, a form confirmation page, or a purchase confirmation page on a non-Amazon storefront. It requires the advertiser to accept Amazon's measurement terms and to generate the pixel from within the Amazon Ads console or through a tag manager integration. A campaign running without the pixel has no conversion signal to optimize toward and reports zero conversions regardless of actual performance.

A few signs point to the pixel being missing or misfiring:
  • Amazon DSP conversion reporting shows zero conversions throughout a completed flight
  • The advertiser cannot point to an Amazon Ad Tag event in their tag management or network traffic for the conversion page
  • The conversion goal in the order settings is defined but conversion volume in the report is zero
You may want to verify the pixel fires on the actual conversion page by inspecting network requests, rather than confirming only that a tag manager container is present on the page.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a non-endemic brand with a direct-to-consumer store, the Amazon Ad Tag belongs on the purchase confirmation page, not the product page or cart. A pixel placed on an earlier page will report inflated conversions that include visitors who did not complete a purchase.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a non-endemic brand capturing leads, the Amazon Ad Tag belongs on the form confirmation or thank-you page that renders after a form submission completes. Placing it on the form page itself counts page views rather than submissions.

learn_more.source_title: Introduction to Amazon Attribution and campaign reporting
A complete guide to Amazon Marketing Cloud

investigate.intro: Confirm the Amazon Ad Tag pixel is present on each conversion page for the non-endemic campaign, that it fires on conversion completion rather than on page load, and that it appears in conversion reporting.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the order for the non-endemic campaign and review the conversion goal configuration. Note whether a conversion event is defined and which pages are designated as conversion pages.
In the order or advertiser settings, locate the Amazon Ad Tag setup. Confirm a pixel has been generated and that the measurement terms have been accepted.

Review the conversion column in the line item performance report. If the campaign has been live for more than a few days and the column shows zero conversions with confirmed impressions and clicks, the pixel is likely missing or misfiring.

browser_devtools.label: Browser DevTools (Network panel)
steps: Load the conversion page in a browser and open DevTools, then navigate to the Network panel.
Complete the conversion action, such as submitting the form or reaching the confirmation page.

Filter network requests for 'amazon-adsystem' or 'ad.doubleclick' to check for the Amazon Ad Tag request firing on conversion completion. A request that fires on page load rather than on the conversion action indicates the pixel is placed incorrectly.

evaluate.logic: For each non-endemic campaign, the practitioner records whether the Amazon Ad Tag pixel is present on the conversion page, whether it fires on conversion completion as observed in DevTools, and whether the conversion column in the performance report shows non-zero data. A campaign where the pixel fires correctly on conversion completion and the report shows conversion data passes. A campaign where the pixel is absent, fires on the wrong event, or the report shows zero conversions throughout an active flight is a fail recorded with the order name and the observation from DevTools and reporting.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the pixel presence, the DevTools observation, and the conversion column value for each non-endemic campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the Amazon Ad Tag pixel is not correctly implemented on {{site_name}}'s non-endemic campaign conversion pages. Without the pixel firing on the conversion event, Amazon DSP has no signal to optimize toward and the campaign's performance report shows zero conversions, making it impossible to assess whether the spend drove off-Amazon outcomes. {{voice_recommend}} confirming the pixel is placed on the conversion confirmation page rather than the product or form page, verifying it fires on conversion completion using browser DevTools, and checking that the measurement terms have been accepted in the Amazon Ads console. Once the pixel is confirmed firing correctly, the conversion column in reporting should begin populating within the next reporting window.

audit_checks.title: Promoted ASINs are not mapped to line items driving retail conversions
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without promoted ASIN mapping, purchase conversions may attribute to unrelated products in the advertiser's catalog rather than to the specific ASINs the campaign is built to promote. New-to-brand purchase metrics, which require the promoted ASIN to be in scope, become unreliable or absent, reducing the value of the conversion data the campaign produces.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP attributes purchases to advertisers based on the ASINs associated with the advertiser account and the promoted ASIN configuration at the order or line item level. When promoted ASINs are defined, Amazon DSP scopes its conversion reporting to those specific products, which means a purchase of an unrelated product in the advertiser's catalog does not inflate the conversion count, and new-to-brand (NTB) purchase metrics apply specifically to the promoted items.

When promoted ASINs are absent or set to the entire catalog without filtering, two problems follow. First, any purchase from the advertiser's Amazon store can credit as a conversion, regardless of whether the campaign drove that purchase, which inflates reported ROAS and obscures which products the campaign actually moved. Second, NTB metrics, which identify purchasers who have not bought from the brand in the prior year, require a defined promoted ASIN scope. Without that scope, NTB data is either absent from reporting or unreliable as a measure of audience expansion.

A few signs suggest the ASIN mapping may be absent or too broad:
  • Reported purchase conversions span a much wider product range than the creative or targeting would suggest
  • New-to-brand purchase metrics are missing from the performance report despite the campaign having run long enough to accumulate data
  • The order's ASIN configuration shows all catalog products selected rather than a curated promoted set
You may want to confirm the promoted ASIN list matches the products featured in the creative, particularly when running multiple orders for different product lines from the same advertiser account.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a brand with a large Amazon catalog, promoted ASIN mapping is worth reviewing at the order level before launch. A campaign promoting a seasonal product line should have those specific ASINs in scope rather than the full catalog, so that NTB data reflects new buyers of the promoted products rather than the brand as a whole.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: Non-endemic advertisers do not list products on Amazon.com and therefore do not use ASIN mapping. This check applies only to endemic advertisers with products available for purchase on Amazon.com.

learn_more.source_title: Introduction to Amazon Attribution and campaign reporting
A complete guide to Amazon Marketing Cloud

investigate.intro: Confirm that the promoted ASINs configured at the order or line item level match the products the campaign's creative promotes, and that new-to-brand purchase data appears in reporting.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the order for the endemic advertiser campaign and locate the promoted ASIN configuration. Note whether specific ASINs are listed or whether the setting defaults to the entire advertiser catalog.

Compare the listed ASINs against the product detail pages the creative links to. If the creative features product A but the ASIN list contains the full catalog, the conversion scope is broader than the campaign's actual promotion.

Open the performance report for the order and check whether the new-to-brand purchase metric column is populated. An absence of NTB data when purchase conversions are present suggests the promoted ASIN scope is not defined narrowly enough to produce that metric.

amazon_marketing_cloud.label: Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC)
steps: If the advertiser has access to Amazon Marketing Cloud, run a query on purchase event data segmented by ASIN to see whether the purchase conversions attributing to the campaign span the expected ASINs or a broader set.
Compare the ASIN distribution in the AMC output against the promoted product list. A long tail of ASINs that are not in the creative suggests the conversion scope is too wide.

evaluate.logic: For each endemic advertiser order, the practitioner records whether promoted ASINs are explicitly listed (rather than defaulting to the full catalog), whether the listed ASINs correspond to the products in the campaign's creative, and whether new-to-brand purchase data appears in reporting. An order with a curated promoted ASIN list that matches the creative and shows NTB purchase data passes. An order with no ASIN list, a full-catalog selection, or an ASIN list that does not match the promoted products is a fail recorded with the order name and the observation about the mismatch.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the promoted ASIN configuration and whether new-to-brand purchase data appears in the report for each endemic advertiser order above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the promoted ASIN mapping on {{site_name}}'s Amazon DSP endemic campaigns is absent or set to the full advertiser catalog rather than the specific products the campaign promotes. This inflates reported purchase conversions by crediting unrelated catalog purchases and prevents new-to-brand purchase metrics from reflecting audience expansion for the actual promoted products. {{voice_recommend}} opening each affected order in the Amazon DSP console, updating the promoted ASIN configuration to include only the ASINs featured in the campaign's creative, and confirming that new-to-brand purchase metrics appear in the performance report in the next reporting window.
